name: Qadiriyya
description: Founded by Abdul Qadir Gilani of Baghdad (1077–1166). The oldest and most geographically widespread Sufi order, spanning the Arab world, Africa, South Asia, and beyond.

name: Naqshbandiyya
description: Traced to Baha-ud-Din Naqshband of Bukhara (1318–1389). Emphasis on silent dhikr, sobriety, and maintenance of spiritual awareness in ordinary life.

name: Chishtiyya
description: Founded by Moinuddin Chishti of Ajmer (1141–1230). Known for openness, love, and the transformative use of sama.

name: Shadhiliyya
description: Founded by Abu al-Hasan al-Shadhili of North Africa (1196–1258). Characterized by intellectual mysticism and integration of Sufi practice with ordinary life.

name: Suhrawardiyya
description: Transmitted through Abu Hafs Umar al-Suhrawardi (1145–1234). Significant in Persia, Iraq, and South Asia.

name: Rifaiyya
description: Founded by Ahmad al-Rifa'i of Iraq (1118–1182). Known for intense devotional practice.

name: Kubrawiyya
description: Founded by Najmuddin Kubra of Khwarazm (1145–1221). Emphasis on visionary experiences and light phenomena.

name: Mevleviyya
description: The order of the Whirling Dervishes, institutionalized around the legacy of Jalaluddin Rumi (1207–1273).

name: Yasawiyya
description: Founded by Ahmad Yasawi of Turkestan (c. 1093–1166). The earliest Turkic Sufi order.

name: Bektashiyya
description: Associated with Haji Bektash Veli of Anatolia (1209–1271). Syncretic order with esoteric tendencies.

name: Tijaniyya
description: Founded by Ahmad al-Tijani of Algeria (1737–1815). Claims direct prophetic authorization.

name: Sanusiyya
description: Founded by Muhammad ibn Ali al-Sanusi (1787–1859). A reformist order emphasizing Quran and Sunnah.

name: Mujaddidiyya
description: Branches from Naqshbandiyya, founded by Shah Waliullah's sons in 18th century India.

name: Khalidiyya
description: Branch of Naqshbandiyya founded by Omar al-Khalidi in 18th century.
